what_it_does: A static HTML/CSS design mockup of the desktop 'B3 Walkthrough' view, where the RHM (right-hand-man) agent drives the canvas camera to a specific node, dims the rest of the canvas with a spotlight vignette, shows a ghost flight path, and presents a framed value-choice card with operator-only verdict controls (approve, reject, decide, comment, skip) plus a comment/voice capture field.
observations: Header contains a brand label, a 'walkthrough' presence dial, and toolbar verbs (layers, wire, force, run).
Left rail shows a node palette grouped into two kinds: Process and Content.
Canvas uses a radial-gradient spotlight centered at fixed coordinates to dim everything except the target node.
An SVG 'flight' path with a dashed, fading stroke and arrow marker visualizes the camera movement from off-frame to the target.
A 'RHM drove the view here' badge is pinned above the target node.
Target node is an 'ask' process node with status 'ran' and address run://demo/ask; neighbor nodes are dimmed.
Footer strip shows live session status: item 2/4, view driven to run://demo/ask, gate open awaiting verdict.

Right panel hosts the RHM tab with N-of-M indicator, session progress ticks, co-presence line, COA options, resolver chip, verdict buttons, and a 'why' capture field with a push-to-talk mic affordance.
HTML comments extensively reference backend/frontend code locations (suite.py, App.tsx) and feature inventory items (WALK-present, WALK-next, WALK-uiresolve, RHM-copresence).
A mobile tabbar is included but intended to be hidden on desktop via external CSS.
standouts: The 'keystone' visual pattern: spotlight vignette + ghost flight path + 'drove the view here' badge makes agent-driven camera movement explicit.
A visible ui:// resolver chip showing ui://node/run/demo/ask → camera/data-ui-ref, rendering the resolution mechanism in the UI.
Operator-only verdict vocabulary (approve, reject, decide, comment, skip) and an idempotency note.
Co-presence line stating the RHM already sees the selected node and its value, justifying the driven view.
conventions: Follows the shared design-system.css variable naming and app grid layout (top/rail/canvas/foot/panel).
Uses data-ui-ref attributes as UI registry handles for nodes and controls.
Adheres to the in-universe vocabulary of two node kinds (process, content) and statuses (idle, ran, cached, stuck).
Breaks typical mockup minimalism by embedding dense implementation comments and code references directly in the HTML.
concerns: Spotlight gradient center and node positions are hardcoded in pixels, so the mockup is not responsive to different viewports or target locations.
The SVG flight path uses static coordinates that would not automatically align with a real target node position.
Several HTML classes (.app, .btn, .bar, .body, .pill, .addr, .sdot, .port, .tabbar) are not defined in this file and depend on external CSS.
The 'Next' button is exposed in the verdict UI even though comments say the gate→fire→advance mechanic stays backstage.
The mobile tabbar is present without a matching media query in this file, so its visibility relies on design-system.css.
notes: A design mockup that turns the RHM-driven camera movement into a visible, explainable UI pattern with constrained operator verdicts.
questions: Where are the JavaScript handlers for verdict buttons, voice push-to-talk, and camera flight animation?
How does the real implementation compute the spotlight gradient center from the target node's actual position?
Is the 'Next' button a resolve verb or a separate navigation action, and how does it relate to the backstage gate→fire→advance mechanic?
What does the top-bar 'run' button do when the RHM is currently driving the view?
How are the data-ui-ref values registered and validated against the UI_REGISTRY in the production code?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the content: it is a mockup of the B3 walkthrough desktop view that visualizes the RHM driving the view, presenting a COA, and collecting structured verdicts.
intention_signals: The file is trying to demonstrate a trustworthy, agent-assisted review flow where the RHM can take control of the viewport, explain why it moved the view, offer framed choices, and capture explicit human verdicts while surfacing the underlying ui:// resolution mechanism.
novelty: The explicit in-UI visualization of a ui:// resolver acting and a 'ghost flight path' as a pattern for agent-driven camera movement.
contribution: A concrete visual vocabulary for agent-driven walkthroughs, including the spotlight vignette, camera-flight ghost path, co-presence framing, operator-only verdict controls, and a visible ui:// resolver chip.
summary_for_embedding: A desktop HTML mockup of an RHM-driven walkthrough view with spotlight, flight-path, COA card, and operator-only verdict controls.
intention_for_embedding: To visualize a design where an AI assistant drives the user's viewport through a gated review and captures structured human verdicts.
